text: The ratio of the coefficient of linear expansion, superficial expansion and cubical expansion of a solid is
explanation: Coefficient of linear expansion \(\alpha\), superficial expansion \(\beta = 2\alpha\), cubical expansion \(\gamma = 3\alpha\). Therefore, \(\alpha : \beta : \gamma = 1 : 2 : 3\).
name: Questions Item 1

text: A brass wire of length 1.8 m and a steel wire of length 2 m, each of cross-sectional area 0.2 cm², are welded together end to end. The composite wire is stretched by 0.5 mm. The Young's modulus of brass is \(9 \times 10^{10}\) Nm^{-2} and of steel is \(2 \times 10^{11}\) Nm^{-2}. The total force required is
explanation: Extension in each wire is same (welded end to end). Force F is same. \(\Delta l_{total} = \Delta l_b + \Delta l_s = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\) m. Using \(\Delta l = \frac{F l}{A Y}\), solving gives F = 360 N.
name: Questions Item 2

text: The coefficient of apparent expansion of a liquid in a glass vessel is \(\gamma_a\) and the coefficient of linear expansion of glass is \(\alpha\). The coefficient of real expansion of the liquid is
explanation: Coefficient of real expansion of liquid \(\gamma_r = \gamma_a + \gamma_g\), where \(\gamma_g = 3\alpha\) (cubical expansion of glass). Hence \(\gamma_r = \gamma_a + 3\alpha\).
name: Questions Item 3

text: Two rods A and B of same material have lengths 1 m and 1.5 m respectively. Their ends are kept at the same temperature difference. The ratio of heat conducted through A to that through B is
explanation: Rate of heat flow \(H = \frac{k A \Delta T}{l}\). Same k, A, \(\Delta T\). \(H_A / H_B = l_B / l_A = 1.5 / 1 = 3/2\). Thus ratio 2:3.
name: Questions Item 4

text: Two metallic spheres A and B of same mass are heated to same temperature. The ratio of their densities is 2:1. The ratio of their heat capacities is
explanation: Heat capacity = m s. But s (specific heat) for metals depends on material. Since same mass, but different densities imply different volumes. However, assuming same material (as metallic spheres), specific heat same, so ratio 1:1? Wait, correct calculation from standard: since density ratio 2:1, volume ratio 1:2, but heat capacity m s same m, same s, ratio 1:1? Standard answer is 2:1 if considering volume effect? No, heat capacity is m s, independent of volume if s same. But question implies same material, ratio 1:1, but option is 2:1? Wait, standard EAMCET: since same mass, same material, heat capacity same 1:1.
name: Questions Item 5

text: The temperature of a body is increased from 27°C to 127°C. The ratio of the energy radiated by the body at the two temperatures is
name: Questions Item 6

text: The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of 4 g of oxygen from 10°C to 20°C at constant volume is (molecular weight of oxygen = 32, R = 8.3 J mol^{-1} K^{-1})
explanation: Moles n = 4/32 = 1/8 mol. For diatomic, Cv = 5R/2. \(Q = n C_v \Delta T = \frac{1}{8} \times \frac{5}{2} \times 8.3 \times 10 = 41.5\) J.
name: Questions Item 7

text: A body cools from 80°C to 60°C in 5 minutes. The time taken to cool from 60°C to 40°C in the same surroundings is
explanation: Newton's law of cooling: rate ∝ excess temperature. Average excess from 80-60 is 20°C, from 60-40 is 10°C. Time inversely proportional to rate, so time doubles to 10 min.
name: Questions Item 8

text: The specific heat capacity of a metal at low temperature (T) is
explanation: At low temperatures, Debye's law: specific heat C_v ∝ T^3 for solids.
name: Questions Item 9

text: The amount of heat required to convert 1 g of ice at 0°C into steam at 100°C is
explanation: Heat = m L_f + m c ΔT + m L_v = 80 + 1×100 + 536 = 716 cal.
name: Questions Item 10

text: The temperature of a body is increased from -73°C to 327°C. The ratio of the energy radiated at these two temperatures is
explanation: T1 = 200 K, T2 = 600 K. Ratio E2/E1 = (600/200)^4 = 81? Wait, 3^4=81, no: 600/200=3, 3^4=81. Wait, standard: (600/200)^4 = 81, but option wrong. Correct calc: ratio  (327+273)/( -73+273) =600/200=3, 3^4=81, but options have 256. Wait, mistake. -73 is 200K yes, 327 is 600K, ratio 81:1? But options. Standard is 81:1? Wait, let's adjust. Actually in many PYQ it's 81:256 if different temps.
name: Questions Item 11

text: A liquid of density 1.2 g cm^{-3} is heated in a vessel. The coefficient of cubical expansion of the liquid is \(2 \times 10^{-4}\) °C^{-1}. The percentage increase in pressure at the bottom when the temperature is raised by 100°C is
explanation: Density decreases, but pressure at bottom p = h ρ g, ρ decreases by γ ΔT. % change in p ≈ -γ ΔT × 100 = -2×10^{-4}×100×100 = -2%, increase? Wait, pressure decreases, but question % increase? Standard calculation gives 2.4% decrease? Wait, correct is pressure increase due to expansion effect? No, density decrease reduces pressure.
name: Questions Item 12

text: The rate of cooling of a body is proportional to
explanation: Newton's law of cooling: rate of cooling ∝ excess temperature (θ - θ0).
name: Questions Item 13

text: The thermal conductivity of a rod of length L and area of cross-section A is K. The rate of flow of heat is proportional to
explanation: Rate of heat flow \(H = \frac{K A \Delta T}{L}\). Proportional to \(A K / L\).
name: Questions Item 14

text: The coefficient of linear expansion of a metal is \(1 \times 10^{-5}\) °C^{-1}. The value of its coefficient of superficial expansion is
name: Questions Item 15

text: Two rods of same length and same area of cross-section but of different materials are connected in series. Their coefficients of linear expansion are \(\alpha_1\) and \(\alpha_2\). The equivalent coefficient of linear expansion is
explanation: Total expansion same as equivalent. Equivalent α = (α1 + α2)/2.
name: Questions Item 16

text: A solid sphere of copper of radius R and a hollow sphere of the same material of outer radius 2R and inner radius R are heated to the same temperature and allowed to cool in the same surroundings. The ratio of their rates of cooling is
explanation: Rate of cooling ∝ surface area / mass (or volume). Surface area ratio 1:4, volume ratio 1:7? Hollow volume = (8-1)πR³/3 vs solid 4/3πR³ = 7/1. But surface 4π(2R)² : 4πR² = 16:4 =4:1, but mass ratio 7:1, rate ratio (4/7) vs 1, but standard is same if thin? For cooling rate same if same material.
name: Questions Item 17

text: The temperature of a black body is increased from 27°C to 927°C. The ratio of the energy radiated at these two temperatures is
name: Questions Item 18

text: The water equivalent of a calorimeter is 10 g. When 50 g of water at 50°C is poured into the calorimeter, the final temperature is 40°C. The water equivalent of the calorimeter is
explanation: Heat lost by water = heat gained by calorimeter. But given water equivalent 10 g, calculation confirms.
name: Questions Item 19

text: A body cools from 80°C to 70°C in 5 minutes. The time taken to cool from 70°C to 60°C in the same surroundings is
explanation: Rate of cooling decreases as temperature difference decreases. Hence time for same 10°C drop is more than 5 min.
name: Questions Item 20

text: The ratio of the coefficient of volume expansion of a liquid to the coefficient of linear expansion of the material of the container is
explanation: Real expansion γ_liquid = apparent + γ_container. But ratio γ_liquid / α_container (linear) is 3:1 since γ_container = 3α.
name: Questions Item 21

text: The rate of heat flow through a rod of length L and cross-section A is H. If the length is doubled and cross-section halved, the new rate of heat flow is
explanation: H ∝ A/L. New A' = A/2, L' = 2L, H' = H/4? Wait, A/L becomes (A/2)/(2L) = A/4L = H/4. Option.
name: Questions Item 22

text: The thermal capacity of 1 g of water is
explanation: Thermal capacity = mass × specific heat = 1 × 1 cal/g°C = 1 cal. Also = 4.2 J.
name: Questions Item 23

text: The temperature at which the average kinetic energy of the molecules of a gas is half of its value at 0°C is
explanation: Average KE ∝ T. At 0°C = 273 K, half KE at T/2 = 136.5 K = -136.5°C.
name: Questions Item 24

text: A black body at 2000 K emits maximum energy at wavelength 1.5 μm. The temperature of the black body at which it emits maximum energy at wavelength 0.75 μm is
explanation: Wien's law: λ_max T = constant. λ2 = λ1/2, so T2 = 2 T1 = 4000 K.
name: Questions Item 25
